metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay payment service calls 5 downstream APIs: HDFC gateway, VISA network, Aadhaar KYC, SMS gateway, fraud detection. Without bulkhead: HDFC gateway slowdown → all 200 threads consumed waiting → VISA calls also blocked → entire payment service down (even for non-HDFC payments). With bulkhead: HDFC gets 40 threads, VISA gets 40 threads, KYC gets 20 threads → HDFC slowdown is ISOLATED. After this topic, you can prevent cascading failures across your entire service.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay without bulkhead isolation:

```
08:00: HDFC gateway responds in 10s (should be 200ms)
08:01: 40 requests waiting for HDFC → 40 threads stuck
08:02: 80 more requests → 80 threads stuck
08:03: 200 threads exhausted (entire thread pool)
08:04: VISA payments also blocked (VISA is fine, but no threads!)
08:05: Entire payment service: 503 Service Unavailable
08:06: Flipkart, Zomato, Swiggy all down (not HDFC's fault!)

With Bulkhead:
08:00: HDFC gateway 10s → HDFC's 40 threads exhausted
08:01: 41st HDFC request → BulkheadFullException → 429 (HDFC only)
08:01: VISA requests → VISA's 40 threads (all available) → 200ms response
08:01: Flipkart VISA payments: working normally
08:06: HDFC isolated failure, all other gateways healthy
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Resilience4j Bulkhead for thread-pool isolation per gateway, and Kubernetes sidecar pattern documentation.

concept_explanation.bulkhead:
**Resilience4j Bulkhead:**

```java
// application.yml:
resilience4j:
  bulkhead:
    instances:
      hdfc-gateway:
        max-concurrent-calls: 40
        max-wait-duration: 100ms
      visa-network:
        max-concurrent-calls: 40
        max-wait-duration: 100ms
      aadhaar-kyc:
        max-concurrent-calls: 20
        max-wait-duration: 50ms

// Thread-pool bulkhead (heavier, more isolation):
resilience4j:
  thread-pool-bulkhead:
    instances:
      hdfc-gateway:
        core-thread-pool-size: 20
        max-thread-pool-size: 40
        queue-capacity: 10

// Code:
@Bulkhead(name='hdfc-gateway', fallbackMethod='hdfcFallback')
public PaymentResponse callHdfc(PaymentRequest req) {
    return hdfcClient.charge(req); // isolated in HDFC's thread pool
}

public PaymentResponse hdfcFallback(PaymentRequest req, BulkheadFullException ex) {
    return PaymentResponse.failed('HDFC_UNAVAILABLE', 'Try again in 30 seconds');
}
```
concept_explanation.sidecar:
**Kubernetes Sidecar Pattern:**

```yaml
# k8s pod with main container + sidecar containers:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: payment-service         # MAIN container
    image: razorpay/payment:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
  
  - name: fluent-bit              # SIDECAR 1: log aggregation
    image: fluent/fluent-bit:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log
    # Reads logs from payment-service, ships to Elasticsearch
  
  - name: envoy-proxy             # SIDECAR 2: service mesh
    image: envoyproxy/envoy:latest
    # Intercepts all inbound/outbound traffic:
    # - mTLS (mutual TLS) for service-to-service auth
    # - Circuit breaker (in proxy, not app code)
    # - Load balancing, retries
    # - Distributed tracing (adds X-B3-TraceId header)
  
  - name: vault-agent             # SIDECAR 3: secret injection
    image: hashicorp/vault-agent
    # Fetches secrets from Vault, writes to shared volume
    # payment-service reads secrets from filesystem (no Vault SDK needed)
```

visual_aid.description: Two diagrams. Left (Bulkhead): Payment service box with 3 isolated thread pools inside — HDFC Pool (40 threads), VISA Pool (40 threads), KYC Pool (20 threads). Arrow: HDFC gateway slowdown fills HDFC pool, VISA pool untouched. BulkheadFullException shown on 41st HDFC call. Right (Sidecar): Kubernetes Pod box containing 4 containers: payment-service (main), Fluent-Bit sidecar (logs), Envoy proxy sidecar (traffic), Vault Agent sidecar (secrets). Shared volume between all. Network namespace shared: all containers on localhost.
visual_aid.alt_text: Bulkhead: isolated thread pools per downstream. Sidecar: auxiliary containers in same k8s pod

worked_example.scenario: Razorpay payment service with bulkhead isolation for 3 gateways.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Resilience4j @Bulkhead for HDFC, VISA, KYC
2. Fallback methods per gateway
3. @SpringBootTest: HDFC bulkhead full → fallback, VISA unaffected

step_by_step_solution.details: Semaphore bulkhead: limits concurrent calls in the SAME thread (lightweight). Thread-pool bulkhead: gives each dependency its OWN threads (stronger isolation). Use semaphore: for non-blocking I/O (reactive). Use thread-pool: for blocking I/O (RestTemplate, JDBC). Razorpay gateway calls: blocking HTTP → thread-pool bulkhead.

things_to_notice: Bulkhead isolates slow dependencies — not fast ones. If HDFC is healthy, bulkhead has no overhead. Only kicks in when HDFC fills its thread/semaphore limit.
fallbackMethod: must have same return type + same parameters + BulkheadFullException as last param. Spring looks for this method by name + signature.

Sidecar vs Spring library: for cross-cutting concerns (logging, tracing, mTLS) that apply to ALL services, use a sidecar (Envoy, Fluent Bit) instead of adding a Spring library to each service. One change to the sidecar affects all services.
Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd): deploys Envoy as a sidecar automatically to every pod. mTLS, circuit breakers, retries — all in the sidecar, not in app code. App code stays clean.

industry_examples.use_case: 5 payment gateways: HDFC (40 threads), ICICI (40 threads), AXIS (20 threads), VISA-direct (30 threads), RuPay (30 threads). During HDFC NEFT downtime: HDFC threads exhausted → BulkheadFullException → 429 for HDFC requests. ICICI, AXIS, VISA, RuPay: all working normally (isolated bulkheads). Merchant experience: 'HDFC payments temporarily unavailable, try ICICI' — not 'all payments down'.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you prevent cascading failures in a microservice calling multiple downstream APIs?' → Bulkhead pattern. Resilience4j @Bulkhead with separate thread pools per downstream. When one downstream is slow: its threads fill up, BulkheadFullException thrown, fallback returns, other downstreams unaffected. Combine with Circuit Breaker: Bulkhead prevents thread starvation, Circuit Breaker prevents calls when downstream is completely down.
applications.real_world_workflow: Bulkhead workflow: (1) identify downstream dependencies, (2) assign thread/semaphore limits per dependency based on SLA, (3) implement @Bulkhead on each client method, (4) write fallback methods (graceful degradation), (5) monitor: Micrometer bulkhead.available_concurrent_calls gauge, (6) tune limits based on observed traffic patterns.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using SEMAPHORE bulkhead with blocking I/O calls. Semaphore limits concurrent calls but doesn't give the dependency its own threads — slow calls still consume the calling thread.
common_gaps.remediation: Semaphore: use with non-blocking I/O (WebFlux/reactive). Thread-pool: use with blocking I/O (RestTemplate, JDBC, old libraries). Thread-pool isolates at the thread level — even if HDFC is blocked, other threads are in a different pool.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @Bulkhead(name='hdfc', type=Bulkhead.Type.SEMAPHORE) with blocking RestTemplate — semaphore limits concurrency but threads are shared with other bulkheads

key_takeaways: Bulkhead: isolated thread pool per downstream dependency
Cascading failure prevention: HDFC slowdown can't consume VISA threads
Semaphore bulkhead: limits concurrency, same threads
Thread-pool bulkhead: separate threads per dependency (stronger isolation)
Sidecar: auxiliary container in same k8s pod (shared localhost network)
Envoy sidecar: mTLS + circuit breaker + tracing (without app code changes)
Fluent Bit sidecar: log aggregation (without app code changes)
Vault Agent sidecar: secret injection from file (no Vault SDK needed in app)
Service mesh: auto-injects Envoy sidecar to every pod (Istio/Linkerd)

self_check_questions.question: What is the difference between a Bulkhead and a Circuit Breaker, and when do you use each?
self_check_questions.answer: Circuit Breaker: monitors call success/failure rate. When failure rate exceeds threshold (e.g., 50% fail in 10s): OPEN state — stops calling the downstream (fast-fail). After a timeout: HALF-OPEN — lets a few calls through to test recovery. Prevents calling a COMPLETELY FAILED downstream. Bulkhead: limits concurrent calls to a downstream. Prevents one SLOW downstream from consuming all threads. Doesn't care about success/failure rate — only about concurrency limits. When to use each: Bulkhead: downstream is slow (not necessarily failing). Circuit Breaker: downstream is failing (errors or timeouts). Together (best practice): Bulkhead limits concurrent calls; Circuit Breaker stops calls when too many fail. In Resilience4j: use @Bulkhead + @CircuitBreaker together — @CircuitBreaker is outer, @Bulkhead is inner.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 24 is complete! You've mastered CQRS, Event Sourcing, Saga Pattern, Strangler Fig, and Bulkhead/Sidecar. You now have the full System Design Patterns toolkit for senior engineering interviews and production systems.
